title: Today's AI Brief: The Download
summary: Unlocking lithium, controlling Ebola, and AI-assisted diagnoses

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. Let's dive into some interesting developments in the world of technology. A new extraction process could unlock the world's lithium reserves. This is a big deal because lithium is a crucial component in batteries for electric vehicles and renewable energy systems. The current extraction methods are often inefficient and environmentally damaging. A breakthrough in this area could help meet the growing demand for lithium and make sustainable energy solutions more viable.

In another story, Boston Children's Hospital is using OpenAI technology to improve patient care and diagnose rare diseases. They've already used this technology to help diagnose over 40 rare disease cases. This is a great example of how AI can be used to augment human capabilities and improve healthcare outcomes. It's not about replacing doctors, but about giving them a powerful tool to help them do their jobs better.

And finally, researchers are exploring the use of AI to control Ebola. This is a tough one, as Ebola is a highly infectious and deadly disease. But if AI can help us develop more effective treatments or predict outbreaks, that could be a game-changer. I mean, who wouldn't want to see AI help us tackle some of the world's toughest health challenges?

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
